snippets.title: Accessible Japan - Your Guide to Barrier-Free Travel in Japan
snippets.snippet: ## We are your guide to. # Accessible Travel in Japan. Visiting a country far from home can be overwhelming, and finding the right information on accessibility can be a challenge. We’re here to be your one-stop shop for everything you need to enjoy an accessible vacation in Japan. ## We are your guide to. ## Accessible Travel Basics. ## Accessible Hotels. Learn about the accessibility of different forms of transportation – from buses to bullet trains – and how to book accessible taxis for your journey. #### Osaka Without Obstacles: A Guide to the City’s Best Accessible Stays. #### Yes, You Can Visit Japan: How to Navigate Tokyo and Beyond with a Disability. #### Traveling to Hakodate in Winter: An Accessibility Guide. We also enjoy learning from others and hearing your stories. The best way to do this is via the Japan group on our website tabifolk — an online community dedicated to making global travel accessible to everyone.

snippets.title: Accessible Japan Tours From USA 2026/2027 | InsideJapan Tours
snippets.snippet: Wheelchair accessible public transport, "barrier-free" hotel rooms and flexible English-speaking guides means you can relax and enjoy the sights from the Tokyo Skytree to the temples of Kyoto. Lorna and Mike travelled with us on a fully wheelchair accessible Japan trip. Wheelchair accessible public transport, "barrier-free" hotel rooms and flexible English-speaking guides means you can relax and enjoy the sights from the Tokyo Skytree to the temples of Kyoto. # Accessible travel in Japan. **Visit our blog here to read all about the travels of Peter & Suzy** who took a tailormade wheelchair accessible trip with InsideJapan Tours. ### Accessible Travel accommodation across Japan. ### Richmond Premier Asakusa International (Accessible Room) (Tokyo). * ### Richmond Premier Asakusa International (Accessible Room) (Tokyo). The hotel has one wheelchair accessible room on the 6th floor. The hotel's wheelchair accessible room is 39sqm with a king bed. The hotel's accessible room is 28sqm and has twin beds that are 122x203 cm. ### Accessible Travel destinations across Japan.

snippets.title: Best Locations for Extended Stays in Japan | JAPAN Monthly Web Magazine | JNTO
snippets.snippet: Japan has so many amazing cities for visitors from other countries to stay for extended periods, in terms of living standard and other important factors for the foreign community.**. **Although it’s a very accessible country overall, these are some of our top picks for best areas in Japan for long-term visitors. Although Osaka and Kyoto are the Kansai area’s more famous cities, with the former offering big-city excitement, nightlife and street food, and the latter being perhaps Japan’s greatest cultural and historical city, Kobe is often overlooked as a fantastic base for long-term exploration of the Kansai area. Fukuoka has thrived as a port city since ancient times, and its location in Southern Japan puts it very close to many locations through the Hakata Port International Terminal —including Shanghai, Taiwan and South Korea (accessible in just over three hours by high-speed ferry). We hope you enjoyed a brief glimpse into seven of Japan’s best cities for long-term stays or slow travel.

snippets.title: The Long-Stay Tourist Visa for Japan - Go! Go! Nihon
snippets.snippet: # The Long-Stay Tourist Visa for Japan: Live & Travel for Up to One Year. ## **Long-stay tourist visa for Japan application requirements**. To get a long-stay tourist visa for Japan, applicants should meet certain criteria and prove that they are well-prepared for a long vacation and can obtain a Certificate of Eligibility (COE). These requirements are designed to ensure that those who apply for the long-stay tourist visa for Japan are financially secure and have access to healthcare during their stay. * **No need to exit and re-enter Japan**: Unlike the temporary visitor visa, which requires you to leave Japan every 90 days, the long-stay tourist visa allows you to stay continuously for six months, and extend the visa up to a year. **How and Where to Apply for a Long Stay Tourist Visa for Japan**. There are two ways to apply for the long stay tourist visa for Japan:. Nihon is partnering with a Japanese immigration lawyer who can assist with the long-stay tourist visa application process in English, while you are in Japan.

Against this climate, you may think of share houses as an ideal solution for getting on the accommodation ladder in Japan – convenient, affordable, and friendly. ## Which companies provide share houses? Oakhouse launched in 1992 and offers a variety of share houses, apartments to rent and self-styled social residences. Fresh Room provide share houses around Tokyo. Sakura House provide share houses around Tokyo. Borderless House provide share houses around Tokyo. |  | Fresh Room Fresh Room provide share houses around Tokyo. ### Follow Japan Travel.

snippets.title: Japan Long Stays | Where to Stay in Tokyo for Three Months
snippets.snippet: As I was looking at my ad performance one day though, it struck me that rental properties like the ones offered by Weave Living would also be great for travelers planning extended stays in Japan. Before that though, I want to quickly cover some of the unique market conditions that contribute to making Weave Living’s apartments my go-to recommendation for anyone looking to stay in Japan for longer than a month. What’s more, just as is the case with Weave Living’s apartments, these units come equipped with most of the facilities that one would need for daily life in Japan. While you’ll need to find someone else to hang out with in your spare time, having your own furnished apartment from Weave Living that is both safe and secure easily beats out the life in a share house for travelers planning an upcoming new adventure in Japan.

snippets.title: Japan on a Budget: Travel Smart Without Breaking Bank
snippets.snippet: # Budget Travel Japan: Complete Guide for Solo Female Travelers. Solo female traveling, okay, but **budget travel in Japan is even better**! Between cheap Air France flight deals, tips for finding affordable Japan trips, and my techniques for saving on every meal, transport, and hotel, I'm going to reveal **how to transform your dream into an accessible adventure** without spending all your money. ## The Basics for Budget Travel in Japan. My personal experience: I explored Tokyo, Kyoto, and Osaka for 15 days with $650 (not including flights), mixing street food and traditional ryokans. ### When to Travel to Japan for Lower Prices? So know that **youth hostels** (2,500-4,000¥/night, $15-25) create a friendly atmosphere perfect for meeting other female otaku travelers, and **capsule hotels** (3,500-6,000¥, $20-35) offer a unique futuristic experience. The Japan Rail Pass (29,650¥ for 7 days, $174) can pay for itself quite quickly for, say, a Tokyo-Kyoto round trip (26,000¥, $153). Working in Japan while traveling saves you from all budget torments! ## FAQ About Budget Travel in Japan.

snippets.title: 16 great ways to see Japan on a budget - Lonely Planet
snippets.snippet: To help you plan, this guide to budget travel in Japan is full of top tips on finding the best-value places to stay, the right transportation and the best places to eat to suit your wallet, and make your yen go further. Visiting large cities such as Tokyo and Kyoto tends to be more expensive than visiting smaller towns and rural areas, and prices soar at peak travel times such as the spring cherry blossom season and Golden Week; book well ahead to avoid paying top rates for rooms and transport during these busy periods. Japan's famous capsule hotels, which offer tiny "rooms" – more like compartments, with just enough space for a bed, a light and a shelf for your phone – provide a budget-friendly place to spend the night. Like the famous Eurail Pass in Europe, the Japan Rail Pass, or JR Pass, is one of the world’s great travel bargains and it's the best way to see a lot of Japan by train if you're on a budget.

snippets.title: Travel Budget - Japan Guide
snippets.snippet: Dormitories and hostels, found in most cities of Japan, typically charge below 5000 yen per night and person. A stay at a ryokan with two meals included typically costs between 15,000 and 40,000 yen per person and night. Breakfast buffets in first-class hotels typically cost at least 3000 yen. Various fast food restaurants, specializing in noodles, curry, domburi or hamburger, also offer relatively filling meals for 800 yen or less. At this level you will have an even larger range of inexpensive restaurants, such as the above-mentioned fast food places, plus restaurants offering lunch sets for around 1000 yen. A lunch at better restaurants costs typically between 1500 and 3000 yen. At this level, you will be able to enjoy a nice dinner at a wide range of fast food and conventional restaurants, including the restaurants found in department stores where meals typically cost between 1000 and 2500 yen, not including alcoholic drinks.

snippets.title: How to Save Money When You Visit Japan (Updated 2026)
snippets.snippet: Traveling in Japan doesn’t need to cost a lot of money. Yet I think train travel is the best way to see the country, so in order to reduce your train costs, purchase a Japan Rail Pass (JR Pass). Compare that to single tickets, where the three-hour journey from Tokyo to Osaka costs around 32,000 JPY (round-trip), yet you can get a 7-day rail pass that includes unlimited travel on JR trains for 50,000 JPY. You can buy a day pass in most cities which gives you unlimited travel for 24 hours for 800–1,200 JPY. If you’re staying in a hostel dorm room, buying a rail pass, eating relatively inexpensive local food like ramen or donburi with the occasional nice meal, and visiting a few attractions and doing a couple of days tours, you could spend between $90–100 USD per day. Be sure to check out the Japan Rail Pass if you’ll be traveling around the country.

Experiences in Japan National Parks Japan Heritage Sustainable Travel in JAPAN. * COVID-19: Practical Information for Traveling to Japan. # GUIDE Cultural Experiences in Japan Get hands on with this selection of Japanese cultural experiences. ## Japan has a rich cultural heritage and you can discover more about it through a range of cultural experiences. Traditional Japanese flower arranging, trying on kimono and taking part in ancient ceremonies are all great ways of learning more about Japan's diverse culture and picking up new skills. Stay the Japanese way in a traditional ryokan. Few sights inspire images of traditional Japan like that of geisha. Wear one around the town or city for a few hours and get a sense for Japan's traditional sartorial style. Japanese Festivals & Events | Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization). snippets.title: Unique Cultural Experiences in Japan - MAHO on Earth ~ Boutique Adventure Tours & Travel (2024)
snippets.snippet: From watching a Kabuki theater to attending a Geisha performance on a small budget, from visiting the Ninja Temple to stepping back in time in Hokkaido, here is my list of the top 10 off the beaten path Japan cultural activities:. The castle alone is a good enough excuse, and even more so, with history and culture perfectly preserved, Gujo Hachiman City was the Japan I’ve always dreamed of visiting, almost like stepping back in time. ### Dress up for a swimming pool photoshoot in Kanazawa’s 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art. Continuing with Kanazawa, near the Kenrouken Garden, you’ll find the Kanazawa Castle and the 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art, which features a swimming pool with people trapped inside like fish in an aquarium. If you happen to be in a city with a Kabuki theater, make sure to immerse yourself in this unique cultural experience in Japan and remember to pay attention to the special make-up of the actors, how men impressively play female roles, the exquisite costumes, the beautiful music, and the dance.

snippets.title: Cultural Activities | Japan Experience
snippets.snippet: From kimono rental to creating your own chopsticks in Kyoto, and unique activities in more remote areas, experience an adventure like no other with Japan Experience activities. Traditional Japanese arts and crafts are world-renowned for attention to detail, making them a cultural staple that dates back centuries. In Tokyo, take a look at our **Washi-making** experience where traditional Japanese paper is created with age-old techniques. Experience a glimpse of Japan's cultural and communal heritage through a variety of cultural events and activities. What are the most popular cultural activities? Japan offers a wide variety of cultural activities, but some are particularly popular with visitors. Are cultural activities in Japan available all year round? Most cultural activities in Japan are available all year round. Similarly, activities such as making wagashi, the traditional Japanese pastry, can be adapted to the seasons with different ingredients and designs depending on the time of year.

snippets.title: Tour Japan: National Treasures Travel Packages & Vacations
snippets.snippet: # Tours. Tour guide was great, cultural experiences were fun and educational, sightseeing was enjoyable. Itinerary was exceptional, excellent variety of activities, Tour Guide very welcoming and most helpful organizing free time experiences we were interested in. Favorite experience- great views of Mt. Fuji- Itinerary was very nice, if not a bit crowded at times- Our guide Tomeo was fantastic- she was the hardest working person in Japan- always prepared with illustrations and explanations of various cultural traditions and events- she went above and beyond the required to insure that everyone was comfortable in their lodging and when sightseeing on their own. My fav was mt Fuji hotel and dinner - it would have been nice to stay another night there I did not expect to pay for free time which was 1/2 of the trip — so many Japanese cultural things COULD have been shone us! Tour guide was great, cultural experiences were fun and educational, sightseeing was enjoyable.

snippets.title: Top 3 Cultural Experiences in Japan You Can’t Miss – The Girl Who Goes
snippets.snippet: Last Updated on October 6, 2025. Now that you know exactly what we got up to during our two weeks in Japan, I wanted to focus on some of the cultural experiences in Japan that really made our trip special. As I was researching the best experiences in Japan, one thing I kept seeing was visiting an “onsen.” An *onsen* is a traditional hot spring in Japan, but most of the time it seems like you book a private one in your hotel or in a guesthouse. In the women’s locker room, I was armed with the instruction paper but I put it in the locker and then I didn’t have access to it anymore. Let me know in the comments! ### Comments. snippets.title: Health & Safety | The Expat's Guide to Japan
snippets.snippet: # Health & Safety. Though everyday life in Japan is very safe, foreign visitors may find using the of healthcare system and emergency services daunting or confusing. The information on these pages will help you navigate healthcare in Japan, and let you know what to do in the case of an emergency or natural disaster. Though everyday life in Japan is very safe, foreign visitors may find using the of healthcare system and emergency services daunting or confusing. The information on these pages will help you navigate healthcare in Japan, and let you know what to do in the case of an emergency or natural disaster. General Crime and Safety With one of the lowest crime rates of all developed nations, Japan is an extremely safe country to live and travel in. Although Japan is modern and safe, it is always useful to be prepared. This chapter provides some background information and useful references for essential services and emergencies. ### Health & Safety Finder.

snippets.title: Travel advice and advisories for Japan - Travel.gc.ca
snippets.snippet: # Japan travel advice. ## Japan travel advice. Following the 2011 incident at the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear power plant, Japanese authorities have placed restrictions, including travel and overnight stay bans, on the plant's surrounding area due to the risk of exposure to radiation. This section has information and advice about health risks you might face when travelling. When travelling in this destination, you might be at risk for diseases that can be prevented with vaccines or medications. Travellers visiting regions with a risk of typhoid, especially those exposed to places with poor sanitation, should speak to a health care professional about vaccination. When travelling, you can reduce your risk of getting or spreading respiratory infections, like the flu or COVID-19, by:. **IMPORTANT:**The two levels below are official Government of Canada Travel Advisories and are issued when the safety and security of Canadians travelling or living in the country or region may be at risk.

snippets.title: Staying Safe in Japan | Japan Safety | Travel Japan | JNTO
snippets.snippet: Japan is an extremely safe country, but, like anywhere else, things happen. Be aware of potential dangers and know what to do in case of an emergency.

snippets.title: Japan - Traveler view | Travelers' Health | CDC
snippets.snippet: | Japanese Encephalitis | Recommended for travelers who   * Are moving to an area with Japanese encephalitis to live * Spend long periods of time, such as a month or more, in areas with Japanese encephalitis * Frequently travel to areas with Japanese encephalitis   Consider vaccination for travelers   * Spending less than a month in areas with Japanese encephalitis but will be doing activities that increase risk of infection, such as visiting rural areas, hiking or camping, or staying in places without air conditioning, screens, or bed nets * Going to areas with Japanese encephalitis who are uncertain of their activities or how long they will be there   Not recommended for travelers planning short-term travel to urban areas or travel to areas with no clear Japanese encephalitis season. | Hantavirus | * Breathing in air or accidentally eating food contaminated with the urine, droppings, or saliva of infected rodents * Bite from an infected rodent * Less commonly, being around someone sick with hantavirus (only occurs with Andes virus) | * Avoid rodents and areas where they live * Avoid sick people | Post-Travel Evaluation to Rule Out Viral Special Pathogen Infection |.
